#!/bin/bash

# Check if AIDE configuration file exists
CONFIG_FILE="/etc/aide/aide.conf"
if [ ! -f "$CONFIG_FILE" ]; then
    echo "AIDE configuration file does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Backup original AIDE configuration file
BACKUP_FILE="${CONFIG_FILE}.bak$(date +%s)"
cp "$CONFIG_FILE" "$BACKUP_FILE"

# Add ACL verification to each ruleset in the AIDE configuration file
while IFS= read -r line; do
    if [[ $line == \*ruleset\* ]]; then
        # Append ACL check to the current ruleset
        sed -i '/^options/a \ acl_check = yes' "$CONFIG_FILE"
    fi
done < "$CONFIG_FILE"

# Verify changes were made
if ! grep -q "acl_check = yes" "$CONFIG_FILE"; then
    echo "Failed to add ACL verification to AIDE configuration."
    # Restore original AIDE configuration file if changes were not applied
    cp "$BACKUP_FILE" "$CONFIG_FILE"
    exit 1
fi

echo "ACL verification added to all AIDE rulesets."

#!/bin/bash

# Set FIPS mode to 2
setenforce 1

# Update system packages
dnf update -y

# Install necessary packages for FIPS compliance
dnf install -y nss-tools libreswan openswan

# Configure NSA cryptography policy
nss-config-gui --setpolicy fips

# Enable and start the NSA cryptography policy
systemctl enable nss-poll.service
systemctl start nss-poll.service

# Set SELinux to enforce mode on reboot
echo "SELINUX=enforce" >> /etc/selinux/config
setenforce 1

# Ensure the system uses FIPS compliant algorithms for SSH
sed -i 's/^#UseDNS yes/UseDNS no/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart sshd

# Ensure the system uses FIPS compliant algorithms for HTTPS
sed -i 's/^SSLProtocol .*/SSLProtocol all -TLSv1.1 -TLSv1.2/' /etc/httpd/conf.d/ssl.conf
systemctl restart httpd

# Ensure the system uses FIPS compliant algorithms for Kerberos
sed -i 's/^kdc_options = .*/kdc_options = -fips/' /etc/krb5.conf
systemctl restart krb5kdc

# Ensure the system uses FIPS compliant algorithms for DNS resolution
sed -i 's/^use_dns = yes/use_dns = no/' /etc/resolv.conf
systemctl restart nscd

# Ensure the system uses FIPS compliant algorithms for SSH keys
ssh-keygen -b 256 -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ecdsa_key -N ""
ssh-keygen -b 256 -f /etc/ssh/ssh_host_ed25519_key -N ""

# Ensure the system uses FIPS compliant algorithms for GPG
gpg --gen-key --default-key-algo RSA --default-key-size 2048

# Ensure the system uses FIPS compliant algorithms for OpenSSL
openssl req -x509 -newkey rsa:4096 -days 3650 -nodes -out /etc/ssl/certs/ca.pem -keyout /etc/ssl/private/ca.key
```

#!/bin/bash

# Load system cryptographic policy
CRYPTO_POLICY="/etc/crypto-policies/backends/system-policy"

# Check if the crypto policy file exists
if [ ! -f "$CRYPTO_POLICY" ]; then
    echo "System cryptographic policy file does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Read the FIPS 140-2 validated ciphers from the system policy
FIPS_CIPHERS=$(grep -oP 'CIPHERS\s*=\s*(.*)' "$CRYPTO_POLICY")

# Check if the FIPS ciphers are already uncommented and in place
if grep -qE '^CIPHERS\s*=\s*#(.*)' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then
    # Uncomment existing FIPS ciphers in sshd_config
    sed -i '/^CIPHERS/s/#\([^\)]*\)/\1/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

# Check if the FIPS ciphers are already set correctly
if grep -qE '^CIPHERS\s*=\s*' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then
    # Set the FIPS ciphers in sshd_config
    echo "CIPHERS=$FIPS_CIPHERS" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
else
    echo "The CIPHERS setting is already correct in sshd_config."
fi

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Define FIPS 140-2 validated MAC algorithms
FIPS_MACS="hmac-sha1-96 hmac-md5-96 hmac-sha256 hmac-sha384 hmac-sha512"

# Check if SSH server is running and get current MAC algorithms
if systemctl is-active --quiet sshd; then
    CURRENT_MACS=$(sshctl -p /run/sshd/config get macs)
else
    # If SSH server is not running, use the default configuration file
    CURRENT_MACS=$(sshctl -p /etc/ssh/sshd_config get macs)
fi

# Remove any non-FIPS MAC algorithms from the current list
for MAC in $CURRENT_MACS; do
    if ! echo "$FIPS_MACS" | grep -q "^$MAC "; then
        sshctl -p /run/sshd/config set macs -d "$MAC"
    fi
done

# Set only FIPS 140-2 validated MAC algorithms
sshctl -p /run/sshd/config set macs "$FIPS_MACS"

# Ensure the configuration is applied and persistent across reboots
systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Set up system policy to hide user list on login screen
gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-delay 0
gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-inhibit-enabled true
gsettings set org.gnome.login-screen disable-user-list true

# Ensure the settings are applied by creating a gsettings schema if it doesn't exist
if ! gsettings get org.gnome.desktop.session idle-inhibit-enabled; then
    gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-inhibit-enabled true
fi

# Create a systemd service to enforce the policy at boot
cat <<EOF > /etc/systemd/system/gdm-hide-users.service
[Unit]
Description=Hide User List on GDM Login Screen

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-delay 0; gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.session idle-inhibit-enabled true; gsettings set org.gnome.login-screen disable-user-list true'

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Enable and start the service to apply the policy at boot
systemctl enable gdm-hide-users.service
systemctl start gdm-hide-users.service

Type=oneshot

[Install]

#!/bin/bash

# Set screensaver activation time to 15 minutes
gsettings set org.gnome.desktop.screensaver activate-timeout 900

# Lock the setting so it cannot be changed by individual users
gsettings set org.gnome.settings-daemon.plugins.media-keys media-key-binder "['org.gnome.desktop.screensaver', 'activate']"

# Ensure the settings are applied system-wide and persistently
dconf write /org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/active true
dconf write /org/gnome/desktop/screensaver/lock-enabled true
```

# Create a local policy to enforce this setting

if [ ! -f "$POLICY" ]; then
    mkdir -p $(dirname "$POLICY")
    dconf-editor --create-db
fi

echo "org.gnome.desktop.screensaver = <<: BOOL true" > "$POLICY"

# Compile the local policy into effect
dconf update

# Ensure the policy is loaded on boot
systemctl enable dconf-service.service

#!/bin/bash

# Check if Ctrl-Alt-Del is already disabled
if grep -qE '^\(Ctrl\|Alt\|Del\)Key' /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf; then
    # If it is, check for the correct setting and lock it
    current_setting=$(grep -oP '^\(Ctrl\|Alt\|Del\)Key\s*=\s*(.*)' /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf | cut -d= -f2)
    if [ "$current_setting" != "false" ]; then
        # If the setting is not false, change it to false and lock the file
        sed -i '/^\(Ctrl\|Alt\|Del\)Key/s/^.*$/\1Key = false/' /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
        chattr +i /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
    fi
else
    # If Ctrl-Alt-Del is not disabled, add the setting and lock the file
    echo "CtrlKey = false" >> /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
    echo "AltKey = false" >> /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
    echo "DelKey = false" >> /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
    chattr +i /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
fi

if grep -qE '^\(Ctrl\|Alt\|Del\)Key' /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf; then

    current_setting=$(grep -oP '^\(Ctrl\|Alt\|Del\)Key\s*=\s*(.*)' /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf | cut -d= -f2)
    if [ "$current_setting" != "false" ]; then

        sed -i '/^\(Ctrl\|Alt\|Del\)Key/s/^.*$/\1Key = false/' /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
        chattr +i /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf
    fi
else

    echo "CtrlKey = false" >> /etc/gnome-session/gnome-session.conf

#!/bin/bash

# Check if sudoers file exists and is writable
if [[ ! -w /etc/sudoers ]]; then
    echo "sudoers file is not writable."
    exit 1
fi

# Set the default timeout to 5 minutes (300 seconds)
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=300

# Read current sudoers configuration
SUDOERS_CONTENT=$(grep '^ Defaults\s+timed\_auth' /etc/sudoers)

# Check if timed_auth is already set and adjust the value if necessary
if [[ $SUDOERS_CONTENT == *$DEFAULT_TIMEOUT* ]]; then
    echo "The default timeout is already set to $DEFAULT_TIMEOUT seconds."
else
    # Replace or add the timed_auth line with the new timeout value
    sed -i '/^ Defaults/s/^.*$/&\nDefaults\ttimed_auth=$DEFAULT_TIMEOUT/' /etc/sudoers
fi

# Check if the configuration was updated
SUDOERS_CONTENT=$(grep '^ Defaults\s+timed\_auth' /etc/sudoers)
if [[ $SUDOERS_CONTENT == *$DEFAULT_TIMEOUT* ]]; then
    echo "The sudoers file has been updated with a default timeout of $DEFAULT_TIMEOUT seconds."
else
    echo "Failed to update the sudoers file with a default timeout of $DEFAULT_TIMEOUT seconds."
    exit 1
fi

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Check if sudoers file exists and is writable
if [[ ! -w /etc/sudoers ]]; then
    echo "sudoers file is not writable."
    exit 1
fi

# Set the default timeout to 5 minutes (300 seconds)
DEFAULT_TIMEOUT=300

# Read current sudoers configuration
SUDOERS_CONTENT=$(grep '^ Defaults\s+timed\_auth' /etc/sudoers)

# Check if timed_auth is already set and adjust the value if necessary
if [[ $SUDOERS_CONTENT == *$DEFAULT_TIMEOUT* ]]; then
    echo "The default timeout is already set to $DEFAULT_TIMEOUT seconds."
else
    # Replace or add the timed_auth line with the new timeout value
    sed -i '/^ Defaults/s/^.*$/&\nDefaults\ttimed_auth=$DEFAULT_TIMEOUT/' /etc/sudoers
fi

# Check if the configuration was updated
SUDOERS_CONTENT=$(grep '^ Defaults\s+timed\_auth' /etc/sudoers)
if [[ $SUDOERS_CONTENT == *$DEFAULT_TIMEOUT* ]]; then
    echo "The sudoers file has been updated with a default timeout of $DEFAULT_TIMEOUT seconds."
else
    echo "Failed to update the sudoers file with a default timeout of $DEFAULT_TIMEOUT seconds."
    exit 1
fi
```

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Ensure the necessary tools for hardware random number generator are installed
if ! rpm -q --quiet haveged; then
    yum install -y haveged
fi

if ! systemctl is-active --quiet haveged; then
    systemctl start haveged
    systemctl enable haveged
fi

if ! systemctl is-active --quiet rngd; then
    systemctl start rngd
    systemctl enable rngd
fi
```

#!/bin/bash

# Remove gssproxy package if it is installed
if rpm -q --queryformat='%{NAME}' gssproxy > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    yum remove -y gssproxy
fi

# Ensure no configuration files are left behind
rm -f /etc/gssproxy/gssproxy.conf

# Remove any system startup entries for gssproxy
systemctl disable --now gssproxy.service
raw_response:
```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Remove gssproxy package if it is installed
if rpm -q --queryformat='%{NAME}' gssproxy > /dev/null 2>&1; then
    yum remove -y gssproxy
fi

# Ensure no configuration files are left behind
rm -f /etc/gssproxy/gssproxy.conf

# Remove any system startup entries for gssproxy
systemctl disable --now gssproxy.service
```

# Set up GPG for signature verification
if ! grep -qE 'gpg: /etc/gpg/gpg.conf' /etc/sysconfig/rpm; then
    echo "gpg: /etc/gpg/gpg.conf" >> /etc/sysconfig/rpm
fi

# Ensure that the rpm command checks signatures by default
if ! grep -qE 'checksig' /etc/sysconfig/rpm; then
    echo "checksig = yes" >> /etc/sysconfig/rpm
fi

# Set up a cron job to check for unsigned packages during installation
CRON_JOB="0 * * * * if rpm --query --whatprovides gpg > /dev/null 2>&1; then /usr/bin/rpm --checksig /var/lib/rpm/$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S); fi"
if ! grep -qE "$CRON_JOB" /etc/cron.d/rpm-check; then
    echo "$CRON_JOB" > /etc/cron.d/rpm-check
fi

# Set the idempotent flag for the script
exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Check if profile-based configuration tool is available
if ! command -v authconfig &> /dev/null; then
    echo "authconfig is not installed. Please install it to proceed."
    exit 1
fi

# Select and activate a supported authentication profile
authconfig --updateall --enableshadow --passminlen=8 --usertypes=local,ldap --kickstart

# Check if the configuration was successful
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "User authentication is now configured through profile-based tool."
else
    echo "Failed to configure user authentication through profile-based tool."
    exit 1
fi

# Define the required login banner content
banner_text="This system is for authorized use only. Use of this system is subject to all applicable local, state and federal laws. All activities of users on this system may be monitored and recorded. Unauthorized access or use is prohibited."

# Check if the file /etc/issue exists
if [ ! -f "/etc/issue" ]; then
    # Create the /etc/issue file with the banner content
    echo "$banner_text" > /etc/issue
else
    # If the file exists, check if the banner text is already set
    current_content=$(cat /etc/issue)
    if [[ "$current_content" != *"$banner_text"* ]]; then
        # Update the existing /etc/issue file with the banner content
        echo "$banner_text" > /etc/issue
    fi
fi

# Check if the file /etc/login.defs exists and set the Banner variable
if [ ! -f "/etc/login.defs" ]; then
    # Create the /etc/login.defs file to include the Banner setting
    echo "BANNER /etc/issue" > /etc/login.defs
else
    # If the file exists, check if the Banner variable is already set
    current_content=$(cat /etc/login.defs)
    banner_setted=false
    while read -r line; do
        if [[ "$line" == "BANNER="* ]]; then
            banner_setted=true
            break
        fi
    done < "/etc/login.defs"
    if [ "$banner_setted" = false ]; then
        # Update the existing /etc/login.defs file to include the Banner setting
        echo "BANNER /etc/issue" >> /etc/login.defs
    fi
fi

    echo "Creating lock file for organization warning banner."

#!/bin/bash

# Set the maximum number of failed login attempts for root
MAX_ATTEMPTS=5

# Set the lockout time in seconds
LOCKOUT_TIME=300

# Edit the authentiation configuration file
AUTH_FILE="/etc/security/pwquality.conf"

# Check if the authentication configuration file exists, if not create it
if [ ! -f "$AUTH_FILE" ]; then
    echo "auth required pam_pwquality.so retry=$MAX_ATTEMPTS" > $AUTH_FILE
else
    # Update the existing configuration
    sed -i '/^retry=/c\retry='$MAX_ATTEMPTS'/' $AUTH_FILE
fi

# Set the lockout time in the authentication configuration file
sed -i '/^lock_time=/c\lock_time='$LOCKOUT_TIME'/' $AUTH_FILE

# Enable pam_tally2 for root account
PAM_TALLY_FILE="/etc/security/tallylog"

# Check if pam_tally2 is already configured for root, if not add it
if ! grep -qE "^session required pam_tally2.so onerr=fail per_root lock_time=" /etc/pam.d/login; then
    echo "session required pam_tally2.so onerr=fail per_root lock_time=$LOCKOUT_TIME" >> /etc/pam.d/login
fi

# Update the shadow configuration to enforce failed attempt lockout
SHADOW_FILE="/etc/shadow"

# Check if the root account already has a lock flag, if not add it
if ! grep -qE '^root:[^!*]:[0-9]*:.*:.*:.*:.*:.*:.*:.*:' /etc/shadow; then
    # Add the lock flag to the end of the shadow file
    echo "root:*:17790:0:99999:7:::" >> $SHADOW_FILE
else
    # Update the existing root account with the lock flag
    sed -i '/^root/ s/:[0-9]*:/&17790:/' $SHADOW_FILE
fi

# Apply the changes to the system
authconfig --update

LOCK_TIME=900 # Lock time in seconds
MAX_ATTEMPTS=3 # Maximum number of failed attempts before locking

if ! grep -q '^pam_tally2.so' /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    echo "pam_tally2.so not found in system-auth, adding it..."
    sed -i '/^session required pam_motd.so/ i session required pam_tally2.so onerr=fail per_user lock_time=900 accounting yes audit quiet' /etc/pam.d/system-auth

        echo "pam_tally2.so onerr=fail per_user lock_time=$LOCK_TIME accounting yes audit quiet max_fail=$MAX_ATTEMPTS" > /etc/security/tallylog/$user
    fi
done

# Ensure the tallylog directory exists and is writable by root
if [ ! -d "/etc/security/tallylog/" ]; then

    chmod 700 /etc/security/tallylog/
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Define a function to check password strength
check_password_strength() {
    local oldpass="$1"
    local newpass="$2"

    # Calculate the number of differing characters
    local diff_chars=$(echo -n "$oldpass" | grep -o . | sort | uniq | wc -l)
    local len_old=${#oldpass}
    local len_new=${#newpass}

    # Check if the length of new password is less than old password
    if [ $len_new -lt $len_old ]; then
        echo "New password must be at least as long as the old password."
        return 1
    fi

    # Check if the number of differing characters is at least 8
    if [ $diff_chars -ge 8 ]; then
        return 0
    else
        echo "New password must differ from the old password by at least 8 characters that were not present in the old password."
        return 1
    fi
}

# Apply the password strength check to all users
for user in $(getent passwd | cut -d: -f1); do
    # Get the current and new passwords for the user
    current_pass=$(chpasswd -S "$user" | cut -d' ' -f2)
    new_pass=$(echo "new password" | chpasswd -E "$user")

    # Check if the new password meets the criteria
    if check_password_strength "$current_pass" "$new_pass"; then
        echo "Password changed for user $user."
    else
        echo "Failed to change password for user $user due to strength requirements."
    fi
done

# Update the system configuration to enforce this policy on future password changes
echo 'password强度最小差异字符数=8' >> /etc/pam.d/common-password

# Fetch all users who have changed their password within the last 24 hours
users=$(chage -l root | grep 'Password was set' | awk '{print $1}' | sort -u)

for user in $users; do
    # Get the current age of the user's password
    passwd_age=$(chage -l "$user" | grep 'Password was set' | awk '{print $4}')
    
    # Convert the age to seconds and check if it is less than 86400 (24 hours)
    if [ "$passwd_age" -lt 86400 ]; then
        # Set the minimum password age to 1 day
        chage -d $(date --date='1 day ago' +%s) "$user"
    fi
done

#!/bin/bash

# Define the maximum number of concurrent sessions allowed per user
MAX_SESSIONS=10

# Loop through all users to set their resource limits
for user in $(getent passwd | cut -d: -f1); do
    # Check if the session limit already exists for this user
    if ! grep -qE "^$user:" /etc/security/limits.d/*.conf; then
        # Create a new file with the user's limits
        echo "$user hard maxlogins $MAX_SESSIONS" > "/etc/security/limits.d/$user.conf"
    else
        # Update the existing file with the new limit
        sed -i "s/^$user:.*/$user hard maxlogins $MAX_SESSIONS/" /etc/security/limits.d/*.conf
    fi
done

# Apply the changes to the current session
ulimit -Hn $MAX_SESSIONS

#!/bin/bash

# Define an array of common groups that interactive users might belong to
common_groups=("users" "wheel" "audio" "video" "games")

# Loop through each user in the system
while IFS= read -r user; do
    # Skip non-interactive users and root
    if [[ "$user" != "root" && $(id -u "$user" &>/dev/null; echo $?) -eq 0 ]]; then
        # Get the home directory of the current user
        homedir=$(eval echo ~$user)
        
        # Loop through each file and directory in the home directory
        while IFS= read -r path; do
            # Skip if the path is a symbolic link or already group-owned by the user's primary group
            if [[ ! -h "$path" && $(stat -c '%G' "$path") != "$(id -g -n "$user")" ]]; then
                # Get the current group ownership of the file/directory
                current_group=$(stat -c '%G' "$path")
                
                # Check if the user is a member of any common groups and set the group ownership accordingly
                for group in "${common_groups[@]}"; do
                    if groups "$user" &>/dev/null | grep -q "^$group "; then
                        # If the file/directory is not owned by the user's primary group, change it to that group
                        if [[ "$(stat -c '%G' "$path")" != "$(id -g -n "$user")" ]]; then
                            chgrp "$user" "$path"
                        fi
                        break
                    fi
                done
            fi
        done < <(find "$homedir" -type f -o -type d)
    fi
done < /etc/passwd

exit 0

    # Skip hidden files and directories
    if [[ "$dir" == .* ]]; then

    # Get the current permissions of the file or directory

    if [ "$perms" -gt "$MIN_PERMS" ]; then
        chmod 740 "$file"

    check_and_set_perms "$HOMEDIR/.bash_profile"

$ModLoad imtcp

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure required directories exist
mkdir -p /etc/pki/tls/certs
mkdir -p /etc/pki/tls/private

# Configure rsyslog to use certificate-based authentication for remote logging
cat <<EOF > /etc/rsyslog.conf.d/remote-logging.conf
$ModLoad imtcp
$InputTCPServerRun 0

$ModLoad imfile
$InputFileTag prefix
$InputFileStateFile /var/run/imfile.state

$ModLoad omfwd
$OMForwardMode active
$OMForwardTarget "ssl://<remote-host>:<port>"
$OMForwardCertChainFile /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca.crt
$OMForwardCertFile /etc/pki/tls/private/localhost.key
$OMForwardVerifyMode 2
$OMForwardDoNotWaitForTLSHandshakeClose on

$ActionQueueType Disk
$ActionQueueSaveOnShutdown on
$ActionQueueMaxDiskSpace 1G
$ActionQueueLowWaterMark 500M
$ActionQueueHighWaterMark 800M

$ActionResumeRetryCount -1

# Start rsyslogd with the new configuration
systemctl restart rsyslog
EOF

# Configure SSL for remote logging
cat <<EOF > /etc/pki/tls/private/localhost.key
<KEY>
EOF

# Generate a self-signed CA certificate and distribute it to the remote host
openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
  -keyout /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca.crt -out /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca.crt \
  -subj "/C=US/ST=State/L=City/O=Organization/CN=CA"

# Copy the CA certificate to the remote host
scp /etc/pki/tls/certs/ca.crt <remote-user>@<remote-host>:/path/to/ca.crt

# Set permissions and ownership for the configuration files
chmod 600 /etc/pki/tls/private/localhost.key
chown root:root /etc/pki/tls/private/localhost.key
chown root:root /etc/rsyslog.conf.d/remote-logging.conf
```

if grep -q 'atm' /etc/initramfs-tools/conf.d/modules; then
    sed -i '/^atm/d' /etc/initramfs-tools/conf.d/modules
fi

# Blacklist ATM module in sysctl.conf to prevent loading at boot
if ! grep -q '^net.atm' /etc/sysctl.conf; then
    echo 'net.atm = 0' >> /etc/sysctl.conf
fi

# Update sysctl settings immediately
sysctl -p

# Add ATM module to the blacklist in /etc/modprobe.d/
cat << EOF > /etc/modprobe.d/atm-blacklist.conf
blacklist atm
EOF

# Ensure the changes persist across reboots by updating the initramfs
update-initramfs -u

exit 0
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define an array of non-root local partitions
non_root_partitions=($(lsblk -no PKNAME /dev | grep -v 'rootfs'))

# Loop through each partition and apply the mount options
for partition in "${non_root_partitions[@]}"; do
  # Check if the partition is already mounted
  if mountpoint -q "$partition"; then
    # Get current mount options for the partition
    current_opts=$(mount | grep "$partition" | awk '{print $5}' | tr ',' ' ')

    # Add 'nosuid,nodiratime' to the existing options if not present
    new_opts="${current_opts},nosuid,nodiratime"
    echo "Setting mount options for $partition: $new_opts"

    # Update the /etc/fstab entry for the partition
    sed -i "/$partition/s,$current_opts,$new_opts," /etc/fstab

    # Recreate the filesystem with new options
    umount "$partition" && mount -o remount,nosuid,nodiratime "$partition"
  else
    echo "Partition $partition is not mounted, skipping."
  fi
done

#!/bin/bash

# Define an array of non-root local partitions
non_root_partitions=($(lsblk -no PKNAME /dev | grep -v 'rootfs'))

# Loop through each partition and apply the mount options
for partition in "${non_root_partitions[@]}"; do
  # Check if the partition is already mounted
  if mountpoint -q "$partition"; then
    # Get current mount options for the partition
    current_opts=$(mount | grep "$partition" | awk '{print $5}' | tr ',' ' ')

    # Add 'nosuid,nodiratime' to the existing options if not present
    new_opts="${current_opts},nosuid,nodiratime"
    echo "Setting mount options for $partition: $new_opts"

    # Update the /etc/fstab entry for the partition
    sed -i "/$partition/s,$current_opts,$new_opts," /etc/fstab

    # Recreate the filesystem with new options
    umount "$partition" && mount -o remount,nosuid,nodiratime "$partition"
  else
    echo "Partition $partition is not mounted, skipping."

done

# Define the object-poisoning parameter value
OBJECT_POISON_VALUE="1"

INSTALLED_KERNELS=$(rpm -qa | grep '^kernel-' | awk '{print $1}')

# Loop through each installed kernel version and set the object-poisoning parameter
for KERNEL in $INSTALLED_KERNELS; do
    # Check if the object-poisoning parameter is already set
    CURRENT_VALUE=$(grep 'object_poison' /boot/config-$KERNEL | grep -oP '\d+$')

    if [ "$CURRENT_VALUE" != "$OBJECT_POISON_VALUE" ]; then

# Update GRUB configuration to make changes persistent across reboots
grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Define the maximum polling interval for NTP servers
MAX_INTERVAL=64

# Check if ntp.conf exists and is writable
if [ ! -w /etc/ntp.conf ]; then
    echo "ntp.conf is not writable, please ensure it has correct permissions."
    exit 1
fi

# Read current configuration from ntp.conf
current_config=$(grep 'poll' /etc/ntp.conf)

# If no poll directive is found or the maximum interval is not defined, add it
if [[ -z "$current_config" || ! "$current_config" =~ "poll $MAX_INTERVAL" ]]; then
    # Backup current ntp.conf
    cp /etc/ntp.conf /etc/ntp.conf.bak

    # Add poll directive with maximum interval for each server in the configuration
    while IFS= read -r line; do
        if [[ "$line" =~ ^server[[:space:]]* ]]; then
            echo "$line poll $MAX_INTERVAL" >> /etc/ntp.conf
        else
            echo "$line" >> /etc/ntp.conf
        fi
    done < /etc/ntp.conf

    # Remove the backup file if it was created
    if [ -f /etc/ntp.conf.bak ]; then
        rm /etc/ntp.conf.bak
    fi
fi

# Restart ntpd to apply changes
systemctl restart ntpd

# Load SSH daemon configuration file
SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"

if [ ! -f "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG" ]; then
    echo "SSH daemon configuration file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Read current value of ClientAliveInterval and ClientAliveCountMax
CLIENT_ALIVE_INTERVAL=$(grep 'ClientAliveInterval' "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG" | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '[:space:]')
CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT_MAX=$(grep 'ClientAliveCountMax' "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG" | awk '{print $2}' | tr -d '[:space:]')

# Set ClientAliveInterval to the desired idle timeout value
IDLE_TIMEOUT=600 # 10 minutes, for example
if [ -z "$CLIENT_ALIVE_INTERVAL" ]; then
    sed -i "/^ClientAliveInterval/a ClientAliveInterval $IDLE_TIMEOUT" "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG"
else
    sed -i "s/^ClientAliveInterval .*/ClientAliveInterval $IDLE_TIMEOUT/" "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG"
fi

# Set ClientAliveCountMax to 0 to drop the connection immediately after idle timeout
if [ -z "$CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT_MAX" ]; then
    sed -i "/^ClientAliveCountMax/a ClientAliveCountMax 0" "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG"
else
    sed -i "s/^ClientAliveCountMax .*/ClientAliveCountMax 0/" "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG"
fi

# Restart SSH daemon to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Define the list of SSH-related files to check
SSH_FILES="/home/*/.ssh/authorized_keys /home/*/.ssh/config"

# Function to check file permissions and ownership
check_ssh_files() {
    for FILE in $SSH_FILES; do
        if [ -e "$FILE" ]; then
            # Check if the file is world-writable
            if [ -w "$FILE" ] && ! [ -w "/root/$FILE" ]; then
                echo "Insecure permissions on: $FILE"
                chmod o-w "$FILE"
            fi
            # Check if the file is owned by root and group root
            if [ "$(stat -c '%U:%G' "$FILE")" != "root:root" ]; then
                echo "Incorrect ownership on: $FILE"
                chown root:root "$FILE"
            fi
        fi
    done
}

# Check SSH-related files for permissions and ownership
check_ssh_files

# Update the sshd_config to enforce these checks at login
if ! grep -qE '^Match User *$' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then
    echo "Match User *" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi
if ! grep -qE '^  AllowTcpWrappers no' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then
    echo "  AllowTcpWrappers no" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi
if ! grep -qE '^  DenyUsers *$' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then
    echo "  DenyUsers *" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

#!/bin/bash

# Get currently connected USB devices
connected_devices=$(lsusb | awk '{print $6}' | cut -d' ' -f1)

# Create an empty policy file if it does not exist
if [ ! -f /etc/udev/rules.d/60-usbguard.rules ]; then
    touch /etc/udev/rules.d/60-usbguard.rules
fi

# Remove existing rules related to USBGuard
sed -i '/USBGUARD/,/^$/d' /etc/udev/rules.d/60-usbguard.rules

# Add new rules for connected devices
for device in $connected_devices; do
    echo "SUBSYSTEM==\"usb\", ACTION==\"add\", ATTR{idVendor}=${device}, ATTR{idProduct}!=0, MODE=\"0666\"" >> /etc/udev/rules.d/60-usbguard.rules
done

# Reload udev rules to apply changes
udevadm control --reload-rules && udevadm trigger

# Ensure the policy persists across reboots by setting up a systemd service
if [ ! -f /etc/systemd/system/usbguard.service ]; then
    cat <<EOF > /etc/systemd/system/usbguard.service
[Unit]
Description=USBGuard Service
Wants=usbguard.target

[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/usr/bin/usbguardctl start
ExecStop=/usr/bin/usbguardctl stop

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF
fi

# Enable and start the USBGuard service
systemctl enable usbguard.service
systemctl start usbguard.service

# Define the queue size
QUEUE_SIZE=8192

# Check if audit configuration file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/audit/auditd.conf ]; then
    echo "auditd.conf not found, creating it."
    touch /etc/audit/auditd.conf
fi

# Set the queue size in the audit configuration file
sed -i '/^QueueLength=/c\QueueLength = '"$QUEUE_SIZE"'/g' /etc/audit/auditd.conf

# Apply the changes to the running system
systemctl restart auditd

# Ensure the setting persists across reboots by creating a systemd drop-in file if it doesn't exist
if [ ! -f /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service.d ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service.d
fi

# Create a drop-in file for auditd to ensure the queue size is set on boot
cat <<EOF > /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service.d/queue-size.conf
[Service]
ExecStartPre=/usr/sbin/auditctl -w /proc/sys/kernel/auditsysq_len -l 8192
EOF

# Apply the changes to the running system
systemctl daemon-reload

#!/bin/bash

# Define the shadow password file path
SHADOW_FILE="/etc/shadow"

# Check if audit rule already exists
if ! grep -q "type=write,attr=change" "/etc/audit/rules.d/*.rules"; then
    # Create a temporary file to store the new audit rules
    TEMP_RULES=$(mktemp)
    
    # Append the necessary audit rules to the temporary file
    echo "-a always,exit -F path=${SHADOW_FILE} -F perm=wa -F key=user-modify" >> "$TEMP_RULES"
    echo "-a always,exit -F path=${SHADOW_FILE} -F perm=w -F key=user-modify" >> "$TEMP_RULES"
    
    # Load the new audit rules
    auditctl -w "${SHADOW_FILE}" -p wa -k user-modify
    auditctl -w "${SHADOW_FILE}" -p w -k user-modify
    
    # Install the temporary file as a permanent rule set
    mv "$TEMP_RULES" "/etc/audit/rules.d/50-shadow.rules"
fi

# Ensure the rules are loaded at boot time
if ! grep -q "action=audit" "/etc/systemd/system/auditd.service.d/99-auditd.conf"; then
    # Create a systemd drop-in file to load the audit rules on boot
    cat <<EOF > /etc/systemd/system/auditd.service.d/99-auditd.conf
[Service]
Action=audit
EOF
fi

# Apply the changes and reload audit configuration
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl restart auditd

    echo -e "\n-w / -p x -k deletion" > /etc/audit/rules.d/99-deletion-audit.rules
fi

# Apply the audit rules
auditctl -l | grep -q '^-w / -p x -k deletion' || auditctl -w / -p x -k deletion

# Ensure the system is configured to record events for UIDs 1000 and above
if ! auditctl -s | grep -q 'uid_min = 1000'; then
    auditctl -s -a always,exit -F uid=1000..65535 -S unlink -k deletion
fi

# Ensure the system is configured to record events for UIDs below 1000
if ! auditctl -s | grep -q 'uid_min = 0'; then
    auditctl -s -a always,exit -F uid=0..999 -S unlink -k deletion
fi

# Ensure the system is configured to record events for all architectures
if ! auditctl -s | grep -q 'arch=b64'; then
    auditctl -s -a always,exit -F arch=b64 -S unlink -k deletion
fi

# Ensure the system is configured to record events for 32-bit architecture
if ! auditctl -s | grep -q 'arch=x86_32'; then
    auditctl -s -a always,exit -F arch=x86_32 -S unlink -k deletion
fi
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define the disk space threshold (in kilobytes) for triggering the action
THRESHOLD=1048576 # 1GB

# Get the current disk usage of the audit log volume
CURRENT_USAGE=$(df /var/log/audit | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $4}')

# Check if the disk usage is above the threshold
if [ "$CURRENT_USAGE" -gt "$THRESHOLD" ]; then
    # Switch to single-user mode and lock the system
    systemctl isolate multi-user.target
fi

# Create a systemd unit file for persistent action on disk full
cat <<EOF > /etc/systemd/system/audit-disk-full-action.service
[Unit]
Description=Switch to single-user mode when audit log volume is full

[Service]
Type=oneshot
ExecStart=/bin/bash -c 'systemctl isolate multi-user.target'

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF

# Enable the systemd unit file to persist across reboots
systemctl enable audit-disk-full-action.service

    # Check if the file exists and has the correct permissions
    if [ -f "$RULE_FILE" ] && [ "$(stat -c "%a" "$RULE_FILE")" != "600" ]; then
        # Change the file permissions to 600 (owner read/write, no group or others)
        chmod 600 "$RULE_FILE"

done
